question_text: Astronomers observe a newly discovered exoplanet orbiting a distant star. The planet's orbital period is 3 years, while Earth's is 1 year. If the gravitational force from the star on the exoplanet is weaker than the Sun's force on Earth (due to greater distance), why doesn't the exoplanet fall into its star?

options.label: A
options.text: The exoplanet has stronger gravity than planets in our solar system

options.label: B
options.text: The weaker gravitational force is balanced by the exoplanet's orbital velocity, keeping it in a stable, larger orbit

options.label: C
options.text: The exoplanet's star is repelling rather than attracting it

options.label: D
options.text: Exoplanets follow different physics rules than solar system planets

correct_answer: The weaker gravitational force is balanced by the exoplanet's orbital velocity, keeping it in a stable, larger orbit
explanation: All orbits require a balance between gravitational attraction and the orbiting object's sideways motion. A weaker gravitational force simply means the planet orbits at a greater distance and with a different velocity, resulting in a longer orbital period (3 years instead of 1 year). The physics is identical to our solar system.
